response: A screening colonoscopy can convert to diagnostic if polyps are removed or biopsies are taken. That can change coverage depending on your plan.
Dr. Shelby often requires a consult when it’s been >3 years or for documentation. Insurance may bill it as a specialist office visit separate from a preventive procedure.
Anesthesia billing is separate from the office/ESC bill. If you can’t reach them, call us and we can help direct you or document payment details.
Dr. Shelby’s professional billing is typically handled through Bass Medical Group. Facility (ESC), anesthesia, and pathology may bill separately.
Some accounts have prior credits that reduce the current balance. If you received a statement that seems off, call us and we’ll verify.
ESC facility balances are separate from the physician and anesthesia bills. If you have an ESC balance, we can help confirm the amount and direct payment options.
Pathology bills separately if biopsies/polyps were sent. Coverage depends on your plan; amounts are often small copays/deductibles.
Many plans have a specialist copay for office visits, even if the colonoscopy itself is preventive. The exact amount depends on your plan.
If the prep isn’t covered, we can often send a coupon to the pharmacy to reduce cost. Final price depends on the specific prep and pharmacy.
Endoscopy codes (e.g., 43239/44361) are typically diagnostic, not preventive. Colonoscopy may be preventive depending on coding and findings.
Colonoscopy may be preventive; endoscopy is typically diagnostic and may have different coverage. If you only want colonoscopy, tell the scheduler and we’ll note it.
Sometimes insurers mistakenly process an in-network claim as out-of-network. We can request reprocessing; you may also need to call your insurer to correct network status.
If you’re self-paying for MRCP, authorization may not be pursued. We can still place the order and coordinate the facility.
Yes—Dr. Shelby often requires a consult to document medical necessity, especially when scheduling repeats or after long gaps in care.
A pre-procedure consult is billed as a specialist office visit (E/M code like 99202) and may not be covered as preventive even if the colonoscopy is preventive.
Some preps can be expensive depending on coverage. If cost is an issue, call us—we may switch prep or apply a coupon depending on the product.
Insurance reprocessing can take a few weeks (often up to ~30 days). If you receive a new EOB/statement, review it and call us if it still looks incorrect.
Endoscopy is usually billed as diagnostic, not preventive. Coverage and copays depend on your plan. We can verify benefits.
You may receive separate bills: facility (ESC), physician/professional (Bass Medical Group), anesthesia, and pathology. Coverage depends on your plan.
Sometimes insurance mistakenly processes an in-network claim as out-of-network. We can request reprocessing, but you may also need to contact your insurer.
Some coupons can reduce certain preps to around $60–$85 depending on the product and pharmacy.
If a prep isn’t covered, we often use a coupon so the patient cost is typically around $70–$85 (varies by pharmacy and prep).
Endoscopy (EGD) is typically coded as diagnostic, not preventive. Colonoscopy may be preventive but can convert to diagnostic if polyps/biopsies occur.
Self-pay is available in some cases. A common consult self-pay quote is around $150, but confirm with the office for your case.
